responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a structural supplier-concentration risk: MitID's trust model depends on Apple and Google's platform-integrity signals, app-distribution controls, and device telemetry, creating both sovereignty and resilience vulnerabilities. The project operates in a constrained political and institutional environment where incumbent operators (MitID, platform vendors) have strong incentives to preserve the status quo, and convenience arguments typically outweigh resilience concerns. The most critical levers are therefore: (1) reframing the problem from ideology to measurable risk (supplier concentration, continuity-of-service exposure); (2) anchoring technical credibility through non-production demonstrators that prove feasibility without threatening incumbent systems; (3) targeting the AltID procurement window before identity-stack dependencies harden; (4) building a coalition that spans civil society, academia, and sympathetic policymakers to overcome institutional inertia; and (5) aligning Danish requirements with emerging EU-level digital identity standards to create external legitimacy and reduce local resistance. These levers navigate the tension between ambitious technical vision and realistic institutional influence—the project cannot displace MitID, but can establish the regulatory and technical basis for platform-neutral fallback access before the next generation of identity architecture becomes entrenched.

levers.name: Resilience and Risk Reframing
levers.consequences: Shifting the narrative from a privacy or ideological critique to a concrete procurement and continuity-of-service risk—supplier concentration, contingency exposure, geopolitical dependencies—makes the problem legible to budget-conscious public-sector stakeholders and procurement officers. This reframing anchors the project's policy engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance bodies. However, it requires careful evidence gathering and institutional patience; a poorly evidenced risk claim will be dismissed as alarmism, while an over-cautious framing will fail to motivate change.
options: Commission an independent external risk assessment from a recognized EU cybersecurity or digital-governance institution that documents MitID's supplier-concentration exposure, platform-integrity dependencies, and continuity-of-service blind spots, positioning it as a formal input to AltID procurement planning and Danish digital-strategy updates.

Develop a detailed contingency-scenario mapping document that walks through specific failure modes (e.g., Apple App Store policy change, Google integrity API deprecation, geopolitical sanctions on US-based platform providers) and quantifies the time and cost implications for Danish public-sector continuity, framed as input to national resilience planning.

Engage sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee to introduce a parliamentary question or formal inquiry requesting that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen assess supplier concentration and platform dependencies in the current identity stack, creating political pressure for a public response.
levers.review_lever: Reframing shifts attention from technology to risk, but the tension lies in timing and credibility: a risk assessment is only persuasive if it comes from an institution already trusted by procurement stakeholders, yet commissioning such an assessment requires early coalition support that itself depends on problem visibility. The options miss the vulnerability that incumbent operators (MitID, platform providers) will frame any supplier-concentration concern as hypothetical or exaggerated, and may actively lobby procurement bodies to dismiss the risk as speculative rather than operational.

levers.name: Coalition Architecture and Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, and sympathetic policymakers creates distributed pressure that reduces the project's dependence on any single institutional champion and increases the credibility of resilience and sovereignty arguments. However, coalition maintenance is costly in time and coordination; member organisations have competing agendas (privacy, competition, innovation), and holding a unified public position while respecting internal differences requires ongoing facilitation and message discipline.
options: Establish a formal multi-stakeholder working group co-hosted by a Danish research institution and a European digital-rights NGO, with written terms of reference, regular public meetings, and published position papers on platform-neutral identity requirements, creating a visible institutional anchor for advocacy.

Recruit respected security researchers and identity-protocol experts as individual public advisors or steering committee members, leveraging their professional credibility to legitimize technical and policy claims while keeping the formal project structure lean and academically anchored.

Initiate a series of public workshops and academic seminars at Danish universities and European digital-governance forums, inviting both internal coalition members and external critics, to build intellectual consensus on feasibility and risks before formal procurement engagement begins.
levers.review_lever: A coalition amplifies voice and legitimacy, but the tension emerges when coalition members have conflicting interests—privacy advocates may demand full decentralization, while procurement stakeholders want minimal disruption. The options miss the risk that a visibly large coalition can be dismissed by institutional decision-makers as a special-interest pressure group, and that maintaining public unity while managing internal disagreement consumes time that could be spent on technical work or direct institutional negotiation.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Window Targeting
levers.consequences: The eIDAS2 wallet rollout (AltID) represents a clear decision point before identity-stack dependencies harden; intervening during procurement planning (phase three, months 17–24) allows the project to shape requirements and fallback-access language before implementation vendors are selected. Timing is critical because post-procurement influence is much weaker. However, AltID governance is fragmented across multiple Danish institutions and EU bodies, with overlapping authorities and unclear decision gates, making it difficult to identify the precise intervention points and decision-makers early in the project.
options: Map the formal procurement and governance timeline for AltID across all relevant Danish authorities (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and connected EU frameworks), identify the specific document and decision gates (e.g., requirements-gathering phase, vendor RFP, architecture approval), and position phase-two demonstrators and feasibility reports as formal inputs to those gates.

Engage directly with AltID governance bodies starting in phase one with a preliminary concept note on fallback-access requirements, establishing the project as a recognized input source and building relationships with procurement officers before formal requirements are drafted.

Propose that AltID procurement documents include an explicit EU eIDAS2 interoperability and resilience requirement (drawn from emerging European Digital Identity Wallet standards) that mandates platform-neutral access paths and certified fallback mechanisms, anchoring the demand in regulatory language rather than national advocacy alone.
levers.review_lever: Targeting the AltID window creates leverage, but the tension is that procurement cycles are opaque, gatekeeping is strong, and late discovery of the actual decision timeline wastes months of preparation. The options miss the likelihood that AltID procurement may already be substantially planned or locked by the time this project gains traction, and that even with advance notice, procurement officers are incentivized to maintain incumbent relationships rather than adopt new untested requirements.

levers.name: Technical Demonstrator Scope and Defensibility
levers.consequences: Building two non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented on GrapheneOS or privacy-respecting Android, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback) produces credible evidence that platform-neutral, standards-based authentication is technically feasible without creating a false impression of production-readiness that incumbent operators can easily attack. However, demonstrators are not products; they must be lean and defensible, because feature creep, productization pressure, or attempts to integrate with live MitID systems will either consume the project budget or undermine credibility by producing something that appears to promise what it cannot deliver.
options: Develop a tightly scoped mobile demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect against a simulated (non-live) Danish public-sector identity provider, published as open-source code with explicit documentation of limitations, non-integration with production systems, and research-grade quality expectations, positioning it as a standards-feasibility proof rather than a product.

Build a browser-based and/or hardware-security-key authentication fallback demonstrator that requires no reliance on mobile OS integrity signals, paired with detailed technical documentation on how such a path could integrate with AltID procurement requirements and EU eIDAS2 frameworks.

Commission a third-party security review of both demonstrators from a recognized EU digital-identity certification body or independent security auditor, producing a published report that credibly establishes technical feasibility and security assumptions without making exaggerated claims about production deployability.
levers.review_lever: Demonstrators establish feasibility, but the tension lies between leanness and credibility: a minimal prototype that looks unpolished will be dismissed as proof-of-concept theater, while anything polished enough to impress procurement stakeholders risks being mistaken for a product alternative. The options miss the challenge that demonstrators must work reliably enough to be shown to skeptical policymakers and procurement officers, yet any investment in polish or reliability beyond the absolute minimum is scope creep that distracts from policy work.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and External Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Anchoring Danish platform-neutrality requirements in emerging European Digital Identity Wallet standards and EU-level eIDAS2 frameworks transforms the initiative from a national sovereignty campaign into a compliance and interoperability question, making it harder for incumbent operators to dismiss and easier for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to adopt. EU-level engagement also creates opportunities for the project to influence broader European requirements, reducing the burden of solo Danish advocacy. However, EU standards development is slow, fragmented across multiple working groups, and heavily influenced by large technology vendors, so the project's influence is indirect and outcomes are uncertain.
options: Engage with relevant EU technical working groups and the OpenDESK Digital Identity initiative to propose language on platform-neutral access, certified fallback mechanisms, and reduced platform lock-in, positioning the project's feasibility report and demonstrators as technical evidence supporting those proposals.

Publish a position paper on Danish digital-identity requirements framed as an implementation pathway for emerging EU eIDAS2 interoperability and portability principles, using the European Digital Identity Wallet framework as external validation for platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements.

Initiate bilateral engagement with digital-identity authorities in other EU member states (e.g., Estonia, Netherlands, Germany) that are designing their own eIDAS2 wallet implementations, sharing technical findings and coordinating on platform-neutrality and resilience requirements to build collective pressure for aligned EU standards.
levers.review_lever: EU-level engagement amplifies legitimacy, but the tension is that EU standards processes operate on 18–36 month timelines and are heavily influenced by incumbent vendors, while the AltID procurement window may close much sooner. The options miss the risk that EU standards-setting will stall or be weakened by vendor lobbying, leaving the project with external legitimacy that does not translate into actionable Danish procurement change.

levers.name: Institutional Engagement and Decision-Gate Clarity
levers.consequences: Securing formal written engagement from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or related public-sector bodies early in the project (phase one) establishes the issue as a legitimate input to institutional deliberation and forces stakeholders to produce formal responses rather than ignore the initiative. This engagement becomes a success criterion and a gating condition for phase-two funding, creating accountability. However, institutional responses may be generic, defensive, or non-committal, and securing formal engagement does not guarantee substantive influence on procurement or architecture decisions.
options: Submit a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 3–4 of phase one, requesting a written response on platform dependencies, supplier concentration, and the scope of a formal fallback-access requirement in AltID or future identity-stack updates, treating the response as a gating input for phase-two funding decisions.

Invite the MitID operator and relevant Digitaliseringsstyrelsen officials to a structured stakeholder workshop (month 5–6 of phase one) where the project presents preliminary feasibility findings and solicits formal feedback on blockers, regulatory constraints, and potential integration pathways, documenting all responses.

Establish a formal advisory board or steering committee including a representative from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, one from the MitID operator (if willing), and external security/standards experts, meeting monthly throughout the project and requiring documented positions on platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements.
levers.review_lever: Formal engagement creates accountability and visibility, but the tension is that incumbent institutions may engage performatively—agreeing in principle to study the issue while taking no operational steps to integrate platform-neutrality into actual procurement. The options miss the structural asymmetry: the project team has incentives to claim success when any engagement occurs, while Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID have strong incentives to delay, compartmentalize, or defer the issue to future review cycles that never arrive.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Neutralization and Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Explicitly positioning platform-neutral fallback access as a continuity and resilience layer—not a replacement for MitID—allows the project to avoid direct confrontation with the incumbent operator while still advancing the core requirement. By framing fallback authentication as insurance against platform policy changes or geopolitical disruption, the project can argue that both MitID and a fallback path serve complementary public-interest functions. However, this narrative requires careful credibility work; if the fallback path is perceived as a Trojan horse for MitID displacement, the incumbent operator will mobilize institutional relationships to block it.
options: Develop a formal continuity and resilience framework document that positions fallback authentication explicitly as a certified backup access path for critical public services during platform disruptions, creating a shared interest narrative where the MitID operator benefits from reduced single-point-of-failure risk.

Engage the MitID operator as a technical stakeholder (not a decision-maker) in demonstrator design and feasibility discussions, seeking their input on how a fallback path could interoperate with MitID infrastructure without threatening its primary role, positioning the project as enhancing rather than replacing the existing ecosystem.

Propose that AltID procurement requirements include a formal continuity-of-service and platform-resilience clause that mandates testing and validation of fallback authentication paths, framing this as a standard public-sector operational requirement rather than a critique of MitID.
levers.review_lever: Framing fallback access as continuity rather than replacement reduces incumbent resistance, but the tension is that the MitID operator will still view any fallback path as a competitive threat, since it implicit acknowledges that MitID is not sufficient for long-term resilience. The options miss the likelihood that the incumbent will cooperate tactically on technical details while simultaneously lobbying procurement bodies to exclude fallback-access requirements from formal AltID specifications, leaving the fallback path as optional rather than mandatory.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity system faces structural lock-in to Apple and Google through platform-integrity signals, app-store distribution, and trust-model dependencies that are economically rational for MitID but strategically unsustainable under Denmark's 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty mandate. The project's core tension lies between the incumbent operator's legitimate security conservatism (certification-heavy, platform-coupled) and the state's need for continuity, supplier diversity, and a certified fallback path. The selected levers target the three intervention nodes: (1) reframing the problem from ideology to measurable resilience and procurement risk; (2) building institutional and regulatory pressure before AltID hardens the same dependencies; (3) producing credible technical proof-of-concept that standards-based, platform-neutral designs are feasible. Success depends not on displacing MitID, but on making supplier-neutral requirements explicit in AltID procurement and standards before the 2026–2029 planning window closes.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Trust Anchor Specification
levers.consequences: Defining explicit technical criteria for fallback authentication—such as FIDO2/WebAuthn over HTTP, PKCS#11 hardware tokens, or browser-based OpenID Connect—creates a measurable standard against which future procurement can be evaluated and allows the project to position demonstrators as proof of concept rather than novelty. However, specifying a trust anchor that is genuinely decoupled from app-store distribution or platform attestation requires regulatory clarity on what "platform-neutral" means under eIDAS2 Substantial level, and that clarity does not yet exist in Danish or EU implementation guidance.
options: Define a formal technical specification for fallback authentication that lists acceptable cryptographic bindings, device-binding alternatives to platform attestation, and approved authentication flows, then submit it as a standards proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the European Digital Identity Wallet working group.

Reverse-engineer the security assumptions in MitID's current platform-integrity model and translate them into vendor-agnostic requirements (device tamper-resistance, user-presence binding, revocation timeliness) that any platform or token provider could satisfy, then publish the mapping as a design principle document for future AltID procurement.

Commission an external security firm to produce a "Fallback Authentication Threat Model" that explicitly names the risks MitID's current platform dependency creates and specifies the minimum trust properties a fallback path must maintain, then use that report to justify platform-neutral language in procurement.
levers.review_lever: The tension here is between technical rigor and regulatory clarity: a well-specified trust anchor is only useful if procurement bodies and eIDAS2 authorities actually recognize it as equivalent to or better than platform-attestation-based models. All three options risk producing a specification that is technically sound but remains sidelined as a novelty or research artifact if institutional buyers continue to defer to MitID's existing certification pathway and incumbent operator's resistance to competitive alternatives.

levers.name: Demonstrator Credibility and Scope Boundary
levers.consequences: Credible demonstrators prove that FIDO2 and OpenID Connect workflows can authenticate citizens against a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider without app-store mediation, but only if the scope is explicitly bounded—no feature parity with MitID, no attempt at production integration, and clear labeling of what is simulated vs. real. Scope creep (adding real MitID integration, citizen-facing UX polish, or feature richness) exhausts the budget, delays publication, and invites dismissal as incomplete or unsafe, whereas excessive minimalism (proof-of-concept shell scripts) risks being discounted as not credible enough to influence procurement.
options: Build two targeted demonstrators—one mobile-first (GrapheneOS with FIDO2 over HTTPS) and one browser-based (hardware token with OpenID Connect)—each authenticated against a containerized mock Danish public-sector IdP, with published security assumptions, threat model, and explicit scope boundaries, then make the demonstrators and design docs available under open-source licensing.

Develop a single, highly polished demonstrator that handles a realistic citizen authentication flow (login, consent, attribute release) end-to-end, targeting a minimal but recognizable subset of Danish public services, and undergo independent security review to establish credibility with procurement stakeholders.

Partner with a privacy-focused mobile OS project (GrapheneOS, CalyxOS) to integrate the fallback authentication path into their standard distribution, turning the demonstrator into a persistent, community-maintained reference implementation rather than a time-bound research artifact.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between demonstrator credibility (which requires polish and independent review) and project scope (which is already consuming 4.0 million DKK in phase two). None of the options directly address the risk that MitID operators and incumbent system integrators will dismiss the demonstrators as "not production-ready" regardless of quality—that dismissal is a political choice, not a technical one. Demonstrator credibility alone does not guarantee procurement traction without simultaneous political and regulatory pressure.

levers.name: Regulatory Alignment Sequencing
levers.consequences: Securing formal written feedback from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operator on platform-neutral requirements early (month 6–8) creates a decision record that can later be cited to justify procurement language; waiting until month 18 (phase three) means the AltID architecture may already be locked in or advancing under assumptions inherited from MitID. Early engagement signals institutional seriousness but risks premature rejection if the regulatory climate is not yet receptive, whereas late engagement bypasses early rejection but surrenders influence over architecture.
options: Initiate formal feedback-request meetings with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 3, presenting the platform-dependency risk as a continuity-of-service and supplier-concentration issue tied to the 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty strategy, and explicitly ask for written guidance on whether platform-neutral requirements are in scope for AltID.

Embed a policy advisor in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operating company during phase one to conduct informal stakeholder mapping and identify informal champions for resilience and fallback-path design, then use those relationships to frame formal requests strategically in month 7–8.

Coordinate with sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital-policy committee to lodge written inquiries to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen on platform dependency and continuity, framing the issue as a parliamentary oversight question, then use parliamentary momentum to anchor formal project-led feedback requests in month 5–6.
levers.review_lever: The timing and framing of regulatory engagement shapes institutional receptiveness, but all three options assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is a willing interlocutor rather than a defender of incumbent MitID operator interests. If Digitaliseringsstyrelsen views the project as a threat to MitID certification or operator commercial interests, early engagement may trigger active resistance or stonewalling, whereas parliamentary inquiry may create political cover for informal dialogue but risks branding the project as adversarial rather than collaborative.

levers.name: EU Standards Leverage and Cross-Border Traction
levers.consequences: Aligning the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-path requirements with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS2 implementation guidance creates pressure that applies to Denmark through EU-level harmonization rather than domestic politics alone, and demonstrates that the issue is not parochial but reflects broader European resilience concerns. However, EU working groups move slowly, their output is often non-binding, and they are equally subject to incumbent operator lobbying; timing Danish intervention (phase three, month 18+) may be too late to influence EU wallet architecture decisions already underway.
options: Engage with the European Digital Identity Wallet working group and relevant ETSI technical committees starting in phase two, presenting platform-neutral authentication as a portability and resilience requirement under the Digital Services Act and the European Cyber Diplomacy Strategy, with backing from the project's demonstrators and feasibility report.

Commission a parallel feasibility study on platform-neutral identity in another EU member state (Estonia, Germany, or Netherlands) that is also concerned about digital sovereignty, then jointly propose a transatlantic or intra-EU working group on sovereign digital identity infrastructure that frames platform-neutrality as a shared European capacity rather than a Denmark-specific concern.

Position the project's fallback-authentication model as a solution to the EU's Digital Services Act compliance challenge: platforms must provide users with alternatives to proprietary recommendation algorithms and distribution controls, so digital identity wallets should include platform-neutral fallback paths to satisfy DSA portability requirements.
levers.review_lever: EU-level influence is most credible when grounded in multiple member states' interests and EU legislative priorities (DSA, digital sovereignty, resilience), but the project team's bandwidth and expertise may not extend to sustained EU stakeholder management, especially if phase-two funding constraints limit travel and external coordination. Relying on EU pressure to move Danish procurement is also a slow play; if AltID procurement decisions or architectural design gates close before EU guidance materializes, the project's EU engagement becomes a post-hoc standards contribution rather than a procurement intervention.

levers.name: Civil-Society and Academic Legitimacy Chain
levers.consequences: Assembling a visible coalition of privacy advocates, cybersecurity researchers, contingency-planning professionals, and civil-society organisations creates third-party credibility that independent experts (not the project itself) view platform-neutral fallback paths as a legitimate public-sector need; this shifts the burden of proof onto incumbent operators to justify why fallback paths are unnecessary. However, coalition visibility can also trigger organized incumbent operator response, and if coalition members disagree on technical details or policy framing, the coalition becomes a liability rather than an asset.
options: Publish position statements from leading Danish and European digital-rights organizations (Internet Foundation Denmark, European Digital Rights, Access Now) explicitly calling for platform-neutral Danish digital identity, then coordinate media engagement and parliamentary testimony around the published positions rather than around the project team directly.

Organize a series of structured roundtable discussions (months 3, 7, 12) with researchers, operators, civil-society stakeholders, and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen representatives, documented and published as meeting summaries that show areas of agreement (e.g., resilience and continuity are legitimate concerns) and technical disagreement (platform-neutrality is feasible vs. impractical), then use the consensus areas to anchor policy proposals.

Establish a formal academic consortium (with Aarhus University, Copenhagen Business School, Technical University of Denmark) that commits to publishing at least three peer-reviewed papers on Danish digital identity resilience, platform dependency, and fallback authentication in months 8–24, with commitments to engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and relevant authorities as co-authors or stakeholders.
levers.review_lever: Coalition legitimacy is real, but it is also volatile: if coalition members are drawn into direct public disputes with MitID operators or if they prioritize other policy goals over digital-identity resilience, the coalition fractures. Additionally, civil-society visibility can prompt incumbent operators to preempt criticism by proposing their own (limited) platform-diversity measures, appearing to address the concern while defending the status quo.

levers.name: Budget and Phase-Gate Contingency Structuring
levers.consequences: Explicitly structuring phase gates and contingency budgets (rather than front-loading all resources) allows the project to pivot or de-scope if institutional engagement is weak or procurement influence proves infeasible, and it enforces disciplined decision-making at months 8 and 16. However, contingency and pivot planning requires real-time assessment of progress against soft metrics (institutional receptiveness, coalition depth, procurement momentum) that are inherently ambiguous, and phased funding creates the risk that institutional funders will withdraw support in phase two or three if early momentum is unclear.
options: Structure phase-gate decisions around explicit written feedback from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator on platform-neutral requirements (yes/no to formal consideration) as a go/no-go criteria for phase two, and if feedback is negative, de-scope phase two to demonstrator publication and EU engagement only, with explicit pivot to standards and research outputs rather than procurement influence.

Reserve 0.8 million DKK (8% of total budget) as an explicit "Institutional Engagement Contingency" pool, released only if phase one demonstrates weak formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or procurement bodies; if contingency is deployed, it funds additional policy support, legal review, or parliamentary coordination to accelerate institutional traction in phase two.

Establish a project advisory board including members from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operating company (if willing), civil-society organizations, and Folketinget, meeting quarterly to assess progress against procurement and standards outcomes; if the board identifies zero traction by month 12, the project pivots to pure research and EU standards work, with remaining budget reallocated to publication and coalition-building rather than demonstrator refinement.
levers.review_lever: Phase gates and contingency structuring impose discipline, but they also risk creating a permission structure for de-escalation if incumbent operators or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen actively discourage platform-neutral requirements. An advisory board that includes MitID operators may provide early warning of institutional sentiment, but it can also be used to legitimize a early decision to abandon procurement influence in favor of safer, lower-stakes research output. The contingency itself is only useful if the project team has agreed in advance on what "weak traction" means and is willing to act on it.

levers.name: Fallback-Path Use-Case Framing and Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Reframing fallback authentication not as an ideological alternative to MitID but as a mandatory continuity requirement (what happens if Apple or Google restricts distribution, blocks integrity APIs, or imposes geopolitical sanctions?) converts platform-neutral requirements from a nice-to-have resilience feature into a fiduciary duty for public-sector digital planners. This narrative resonates with contingency officers, insurance and risk-management professionals, and regulators concerned with critical infrastructure. However, over-emphasizing catastrophic scenarios (geopolitical breakdown, platform withdrawal) risks being dismissed as alarmist, and incumbent operators will respond by arguing that platform duopoly is itself a resilience feature (no single point of failure).
options: Develop a scenario-based continuity-planning document ("Essential Services Under Platform Constraint") modeling what happens to Danish banking, tax, healthcare, and welfare access if Apple or Google restricts app distribution or disables integrity APIs due to regulatory action (as with TikTok or Huawei), and present it to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the Ministry of Interior as a risk-management brief rather than a project proposal.

Engage with Danish contingency-planning bodies (Defense, emergency-response agencies, critical-infrastructure regulators) to establish digital identity as a critical-infrastructure continuity requirement, then propose platform-neutral fallback paths as a standard continuity control in national digital-infrastructure risk registers and supply-chain resilience planning.

Frame platform-neutral fallback paths as a defense against platform-provider accountability gaps: if MitID integrity failures occur (API downtime, attestation spoofing, operator negligence), citizens need an alternative path to critical services; position the fallback as a quality-of-service and citizen-rights protection measure rather than as a replacement to MitID.
levers.review_lever: The continuity narrative is powerful because it appeals to institutional risk-aversion and fiduciary duty, but it succeeds only if the framed scenarios are plausible enough to worry regulators without being so extreme that they seem speculative. If the project emphasizes geopolitical scenarios without grounding them in measurable supply-chain or platform-policy precedents, the narrative is vulnerable to dismissal as fear-mongering. Additionally, incumbent operators will argue (not without reason) that a fallback path that is only activated in catastrophic scenarios may itself become brittle or underutilized, creating false confidence or stranded investment.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity system is structurally locked into Apple and Google platform controls—not just for distribution, but for core trust signals and device integrity verification. The project faces a fundamental tension: technical alternatives exist (FIDO2, OpenID Connect, hardware tokens), but institutional inertia, incumbent resistance, and convenience arguments make direct replacement impossible within a single project cycle. The selected levers prioritize the intervention points that matter most: reframing the problem as a resilience and procurement risk (not ideology), building credible but lean technical evidence that alternatives work, securing formal institutional response from authorities, and embedding platform-neutrality language into upcoming AltID and EU wallet standards before the next generation of lock-in hardens. Success depends less on building a perfect product than on making platform-dependence a documented liability that procurement bodies and standards committees cannot ignore.

levers.name: Operator-Capture Risk Narrative and Continuity Framing
levers.consequences: Reframing the issue from 'privacy vs. Apple/Google' to 'what happens if platform policy changes, distribution access is revoked, or push-notification infrastructure becomes unavailable for critical services' shifts the debate from ideology to concrete operational risk. This framing appeals to continuity-of-service stakeholders, procurement officials, and institutional risk managers who are already anxious about supplier concentration and regulatory volatility.
options: Develop and publish a formal continuity-of-service risk assessment documenting what MitID and dependent services lose if iOS or Android app distribution, integrity APIs, or push infrastructure become inaccessible or hostile for 72 hours or more, with case studies from real platform-policy disruptions elsewhere in Europe.

Commission a third-party audit of MitID's architectural dependencies on Apple and Google platform signals, producing a detailed technical report that makes the lock-in explicit and quantifies the number of critical public-sector services affected if either platform withdraws cooperation.

Engage Finanstilsynet and Sikkerhedsstyrelsen to jointly issue a formal letter to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen flagging platform-vendor concentration as a regulatory continuity risk that must be addressed in the next identity-stack evolution, framing the issue as a prudential supervision and civil-continuity concern rather than a policy preference.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that Danish decision-makers acknowledge supplier concentration abstractly, but see no immediate cost to inaction because services work today. This lever assumes that continuity risk language will motivate procurement change, yet it misses the institutional reality that risk assessments are often filed and shelved. The options do not address what governance mechanism or decision-gate will force procurement bodies to act on a risk report, or how to make platform-neutrality a mandatory procurement criterion rather than an advisory note.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Specification Insertion Point
levers.consequences: The eIDAS2 wallet (AltID) is currently in design and early procurement phases; inserting platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into its technical specification and procurement documents now will shape supplier bids and architectural choices before design hardens. Missing this window means the same Apple/Google dependencies replicate into the next generation of identity infrastructure.
options: Prepare a detailed technical amendment to the AltID procurement brief specifying that at least one certified authentication path must not depend on iOS or Android app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push notifications, with explicit reference to FIDO2/WebAuthn and hardware-token fallback options, and submit it formally to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by month 12.

Work with Danish procurement law specialists to draft model language for AltID and related identity contracts requiring bidders to demonstrate platform-neutral and fallback-capable architectures, then place this language in a publicly available toolkit that procurement bodies and ministries can adopt without re-negotiation.

Embed platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into the AltID conformity-assessment criteria and eIDAS2 certification checklist used by Danish and EU validators, ensuring that certified wallets must prove their independence from exclusive platform reliance or face decertification risk.
levers.review_lever: The lever correctly identifies AltID as a critical decision window, but assumes that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will actively solicit or be receptive to external procurement input. In practice, procurement documents are often locked before public consultation, and inserting new requirements mid-cycle creates cost and schedule friction that procurement bodies resist. The options do not account for how to make platform-neutrality a contractual obligation rather than an optional nice-to-have, or how to prevent suppliers from creating a token fallback that is technically compliant but operationally orphaned and unsupported.

levers.name: Hardware-Token and Browser-Based Escape Hatch
levers.consequences: Developing a working non-production demonstrator that authenticates Danish citizens using a hardware token (e.g., YubiKey) or browser-based WebAuthn flow decouples authentication from mobile OS platforms entirely and provides proof that critical public-sector access does not require Apple or Google mediation. This shifts the conversation from 'is it possible?' to 'we have code and users have tried it.'
options: Build and test a browser-based FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication demonstrator integrated with a simulated Danish identity provider, allowing users on any device (desktop, older phone, tablet) with a web browser to authenticate without relying on platform-specific app stores or device-integrity APIs, and publish performance and usability data.

Develop a USB hardware-token authentication path (using FIDO2-compatible tokens such as YubiKey or Titan) that works with a simplified Nordic Public Sector digital identity mock-up, demonstrating that citizens without modern smartphones or those who distrust app platforms have a certified fallback.

Create a privacy-respecting Android authenticator application running on GrapheneOS or /e/OS that demonstrates FIDO2 and OpenID Connect authentication against a mock Danish identity provider without depending on Google Play Services, Play Integrity API, or proprietary push notifications, then publish architectural documentation on how this design avoids platform lock-in.
levers.review_lever: This lever assumes that a working demonstrator will move procurement debates from theoretical objections to practical feasibility. However, a non-production prototype does not address the integration burden on banks, healthcare portals, and tax authorities, nor does it solve the cultural preference for mobile-app convenience. The options produce evidence but do not include a path to pilot adoption in real services, so the demonstrators risk remaining demonstrations rather than becoming operational alternatives that citizens and operators actually trust and use.

levers.name: Academic and Civil-Society Third-Party Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Publishing technical findings and policy proposals through academic and civil-society partners (universities, digital-rights organisations, international security bodies) rather than solely through project channels creates peer-reviewed credibility and political distance from vendor-hostile framing. This makes platform-neutrality appear as a rational institutional position, not a niche complaint.
options: Publish the feasibility study and demonstrator technical findings in peer-reviewed security and digital-governance journals, and present at international conferences (e.g., EIC, IEEE S&P, NDSS) to establish that platform-neutral Danish digital identity is a recognized research problem with credible solutions, not a fringe idea.

Partner with established civil-society organisations such as Internetfonden, Privacy International, and Access Now to co-author policy briefs and co-host public workshops in Copenhagen on digital sovereignty and platform resilience, ensuring that the message appears to come from trusted independent voices rather than from a single project.

Engage academic digital-identity research networks in Denmark, Germany, and Sweden to form a working group that collectively documents platform-dependency risks and fallback-access architectures across Northern Europe, making this a multi-country institutional issue rather than a Denmark-only agenda.
levers.review_lever: The lever relies on the assumption that academic and civil-society credibility will influence procurement and standards decisions, yet policy makers often dismiss academic research as out of touch with operational constraints. The options do not specify how to connect research publication to actual procurement bodies or standards committees, nor do they address the time lag between publication and institutional adoption, which may exceed the 24-month project horizon.

levers.name: EU Wallet Harmonization and Standards Lock-In
levers.consequences: The European Digital Identity Wallet (EUDIW) framework is under active development and will set the template for all member-state identity implementations, including Denmark's AltID. Embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into EU wallet standards now will create a binding constraint that Danish procurement bodies and operators must respect, turning supplier dependence into a technical and legal non-compliance issue.
options: Engage directly with the EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and eIDAS2 standards bodies to propose specific technical requirements for platform-neutral and fallback-capable authentication paths, backed by the project's feasibility report and demonstrators, and secure formal inclusion in the EUDIW implementation guidelines.

Coordinate with other European digital-sovereignty initiatives (France's MoneID, Germany's Gaiax, Sweden's e-ID coalition) to form a multi-country advocacy coalition that demands EU-level platform-neutrality and resilience requirements in the EUDIW standard, making platform lock-in a supra-national compliance issue rather than a national procurement preference.

Participate in and shape the EU's Digital Europe programme and Horizon Europe funding calls to ensure that future wallet and identity-infrastructure grants explicitly require platform-neutral and fallback-capable architectures, creating financial incentives for supplier-independent design rather than Apple/Google mediation.
levers.review_lever: This lever correctly identifies the EU wallet framework as a critical leverage point, but assumes that EU standards-setting bodies will adopt platform-neutrality language when they currently do not prioritize it. The options do not account for the fact that EU standards processes are slow (12–24 months), that many member states and large vendors have entrenched interests in app-based identity, and that 'platform-neutral' may be interpreted narrowly as 'works on both iOS and Android' rather than 'avoids platform dependence altogether.' The options also do not specify how to ensure that platform-neutrality requirements in EU standards are enforced during member-state implementation, rather than treated as optional guidance.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Co-Option
levers.consequences: MitID operator (NemLog-in) and major identity-dependent services (banks, healthcare, tax) may resist fallback-access requirements if they perceive them as operational burden or cost. Engaging them early as co-designers of fallback architectures (rather than as obstacles) transforms them from blockers into implementers and creates buy-in for continuity planning that benefits them as well.
options: Establish a formal Working Group on Identity Resilience and Continuity with NemLog-in, major Danish banks, and healthcare authorities, wherein the project presents the fallback-authentication demonstrators and invites participants to identify operational and integration pathways that do not require wholesale replacement of MitID, but rather add a certified alternative access path for scenarios where platform availability is compromised.

Commission NemLog-in to conduct an internal feasibility assessment of integrating FIDO2/WebAuthn and hardware-token fallback paths into MitID's certification and operation models, positioning the project's demonstrators as a proof-of-concept that informs NemLog-in's own roadmap rather than as external critique.

Offer Danish banks and healthcare providers a pilot programme in which they integrate the project's hardware-token and browser-based fallback authentication paths into their identity-verification workflows for non-critical or supplementary services, generating operational data and user feedback that mitigates institutional reluctance to adopt fallback paths in production.
levers.review_lever: This lever assumes that incumbent operators will engage constructively if positioned as partners rather than as targets of pressure. However, MitID is a profitable, conservative system, and NemLog-in has institutional and commercial incentives to maintain Apple/Google dependence if it reduces their liability and operational burden. The options do not address how to handle the likely scenario in which NemLog-in agrees to engage but uses the process to delay or dilute platform-neutrality requirements, or how to escalate if engagement stalls.

levers.name: Regulatory Mandate Trigger and Compliance Ratchet
levers.consequences: If platform-neutral and fallback-access requirements are embedded in Danish financial regulations (via Finanstilsynet), healthcare system standards (via Sundhedsstyrelsen), or formal government digital-governance mandates, they become mandatory compliance items rather than optional policy suggestions. This creates a bottom-up institutional pressure that procurement bodies cannot ignore without explaining regulatory non-compliance.
options: Work with Finanstilsynet to embed identity-system resilience and platform-neutrality requirements into the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) implementation guidance and bank-specific security regulations, making fallback-access capability a formal supervisory expectation for all banks using digital identity for customer authentication.

Secure a formal government directive or digital-strategy update (in line with the 2026–2029 Digital Welfare Strategy) that mandates all public-sector digital-identity systems procured or certified after 2027 must include platform-neutral and certified fallback-access paths, enforceable via audit and decertification.

Engage Sundhedsstyrelsen and regional health authorities to adopt platform-neutral and fallback-access requirements as a binding criterion in all future healthcare identity and authorization system procurements, positioning digital sovereignty and continuity of care as health-sector imperatives rather than IT preferences.
levers.review_lever: This lever attempts to use regulatory and supervisory authority to override procurement inertia, yet it assumes that regulators will prioritize platform-neutrality when they currently do not and may face political or commercial pressure to avoid imposing costly requirements on operators. The options do not account for the lengthy timelines of regulatory change (12–36 months) or the possibility that incumbent operators and large platforms will lobby against compliance requirements. The options also do not specify what enforcement mechanism or penalty structure will ensure that compliance mandates are actual rather than aspirational.

levers.name: Resilience and Risk Reframing
levers.consequences: Shifting the narrative from a privacy or ideological critique to a concrete procurement and continuity-of-service risk—supplier concentration, contingency exposure, geopolitical dependencies—makes the problem legible to budget-conscious public-sector stakeholders and procurement officers. This reframing anchors the project's policy engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance bodies. However, it requires careful evidence gathering and institutional patience; a poorly evidenced risk claim will be dismissed as alarmism, while an over-cautious framing will fail to motivate change.
options: Commission an independent external risk assessment from a recognized EU cybersecurity or digital-governance institution that documents MitID's supplier-concentration exposure, platform-integrity dependencies, and continuity-of-service blind spots, positioning it as a formal input to AltID procurement planning and Danish digital-strategy updates.

Develop a detailed contingency-scenario mapping document that walks through specific failure modes (e.g., Apple App Store policy change, Google integrity API deprecation, geopolitical sanctions on US-based platform providers) and quantifies the time and cost implications for Danish public-sector continuity, framed as input to national resilience planning.

Engage sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee to introduce a parliamentary question or formal inquiry requesting that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen assess supplier concentration and platform dependencies in the current identity stack, creating political pressure for a public response.
levers.review: Reframing shifts attention from technology to risk, but the tension lies in timing and credibility: a risk assessment is only persuasive if it comes from an institution already trusted by procurement stakeholders, yet commissioning such an assessment requires early coalition support that itself depends on problem visibility. The options miss the vulnerability that incumbent operators (MitID, platform providers) will frame any supplier-concentration concern as hypothetical or exaggerated, and may actively lobby procurement bodies to dismiss the risk as speculative rather than operational.

levers.name: Coalition Architecture and Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, and sympathetic policymakers creates distributed pressure that reduces the project's dependence on any single institutional champion and increases the credibility of resilience and sovereignty arguments. However, coalition maintenance is costly in time and coordination; member organisations have competing agendas (privacy, competition, innovation), and holding a unified public position while respecting internal differences requires ongoing facilitation and message discipline.
options: Establish a formal multi-stakeholder working group co-hosted by a Danish research institution and a European digital-rights NGO, with written terms of reference, regular public meetings, and published position papers on platform-neutral identity requirements, creating a visible institutional anchor for advocacy.

Recruit respected security researchers and identity-protocol experts as individual public advisors or steering committee members, leveraging their professional credibility to legitimize technical and policy claims while keeping the formal project structure lean and academically anchored.

Initiate a series of public workshops and academic seminars at Danish universities and European digital-governance forums, inviting both internal coalition members and external critics, to build intellectual consensus on feasibility and risks before formal procurement engagement begins.
levers.review: A coalition amplifies voice and legitimacy, but the tension emerges when coalition members have conflicting interests—privacy advocates may demand full decentralization, while procurement stakeholders want minimal disruption. The options miss the risk that a visibly large coalition can be dismissed by institutional decision-makers as a special-interest pressure group, and that maintaining public unity while managing internal disagreement consumes time that could be spent on technical work or direct institutional negotiation.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Window Targeting
levers.consequences: The eIDAS2 wallet rollout (AltID) represents a clear decision point before identity-stack dependencies harden; intervening during procurement planning (phase three, months 17–24) allows the project to shape requirements and fallback-access language before implementation vendors are selected. Timing is critical because post-procurement influence is much weaker. However, AltID governance is fragmented across multiple Danish institutions and EU bodies, with overlapping authorities and unclear decision gates, making it difficult to identify the precise intervention points and decision-makers early in the project.
options: Map the formal procurement and governance timeline for AltID across all relevant Danish authorities (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and connected EU frameworks), identify the specific document and decision gates (e.g., requirements-gathering phase, vendor RFP, architecture approval), and position phase-two demonstrators and feasibility reports as formal inputs to those gates.

Engage directly with AltID governance bodies starting in phase one with a preliminary concept note on fallback-access requirements, establishing the project as a recognized input source and building relationships with procurement officers before formal requirements are drafted.

Propose that AltID procurement documents include an explicit EU eIDAS2 interoperability and resilience requirement (drawn from emerging European Digital Identity Wallet standards) that mandates platform-neutral access paths and certified fallback mechanisms, anchoring the demand in regulatory language rather than national advocacy alone.
levers.review: Targeting the AltID window creates leverage, but the tension is that procurement cycles are opaque, gatekeeping is strong, and late discovery of the actual decision timeline wastes months of preparation. The options miss the likelihood that AltID procurement may already be substantially planned or locked by the time this project gains traction, and that even with advance notice, procurement officers are incentivized to maintain incumbent relationships rather than adopt new untested requirements.

levers.name: Technical Demonstrator Scope and Defensibility
levers.consequences: Building two non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented on GrapheneOS or privacy-respecting Android, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback) produces credible evidence that platform-neutral, standards-based authentication is technically feasible without creating a false impression of production-readiness that incumbent operators can easily attack. However, demonstrators are not products; they must be lean and defensible, because feature creep, productization pressure, or attempts to integrate with live MitID systems will either consume the project budget or undermine credibility by producing something that appears to promise what it cannot deliver.
options: Develop a tightly scoped mobile demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect against a simulated (non-live) Danish public-sector identity provider, published as open-source code with explicit documentation of limitations, non-integration with production systems, and research-grade quality expectations, positioning it as a standards-feasibility proof rather than a product.

Build a browser-based and/or hardware-security-key authentication fallback demonstrator that requires no reliance on mobile OS integrity signals, paired with detailed technical documentation on how such a path could integrate with AltID procurement requirements and EU eIDAS2 frameworks.

Commission a third-party security review of both demonstrators from a recognized EU digital-identity certification body or independent security auditor, producing a published report that credibly establishes technical feasibility and security assumptions without making exaggerated claims about production deployability.
levers.review: Demonstrators establish feasibility, but the tension lies between leanness and credibility: a minimal prototype that looks unpolished will be dismissed as proof-of-concept theater, while anything polished enough to impress procurement stakeholders risks being mistaken for a product alternative. The options miss the challenge that demonstrators must work reliably enough to be shown to skeptical policymakers and procurement officers, yet any investment in polish or reliability beyond the absolute minimum is scope creep that distracts from policy work.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and External Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Anchoring Danish platform-neutrality requirements in emerging European Digital Identity Wallet standards and EU-level eIDAS2 frameworks transforms the initiative from a national sovereignty campaign into a compliance and interoperability question, making it harder for incumbent operators to dismiss and easier for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to adopt. EU-level engagement also creates opportunities for the project to influence broader European requirements, reducing the burden of solo Danish advocacy. However, EU standards development is slow, fragmented across multiple working groups, and heavily influenced by large technology vendors, so the project's influence is indirect and outcomes are uncertain.
options: Engage with relevant EU technical working groups and the OpenDESK Digital Identity initiative to propose language on platform-neutral access, certified fallback mechanisms, and reduced platform lock-in, positioning the project's feasibility report and demonstrators as technical evidence supporting those proposals.

Publish a position paper on Danish digital-identity requirements framed as an implementation pathway for emerging EU eIDAS2 interoperability and portability principles, using the European Digital Identity Wallet framework as external validation for platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements.

Initiate bilateral engagement with digital-identity authorities in other EU member states (e.g., Estonia, Netherlands, Germany) that are designing their own eIDAS2 wallet implementations, sharing technical findings and coordinating on platform-neutrality and resilience requirements to build collective pressure for aligned EU standards.
levers.review: EU-level engagement amplifies legitimacy, but the tension is that EU standards processes operate on 18–36 month timelines and are heavily influenced by incumbent vendors, while the AltID procurement window may close much sooner. The options miss the risk that EU standards-setting will stall or be weakened by vendor lobbying, leaving the project with external legitimacy that does not translate into actionable Danish procurement change.

levers.name: Institutional Engagement and Decision-Gate Clarity
levers.consequences: Securing formal written engagement from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or related public-sector bodies early in the project (phase one) establishes the issue as a legitimate input to institutional deliberation and forces stakeholders to produce formal responses rather than ignore the initiative. This engagement becomes a success criterion and a gating condition for phase-two funding, creating accountability. However, institutional responses may be generic, defensive, or non-committal, and securing formal engagement does not guarantee substantive influence on procurement or architecture decisions.
options: Submit a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 3–4 of phase one, requesting a written response on platform dependencies, supplier concentration, and the scope of a formal fallback-access requirement in AltID or future identity-stack updates, treating the response as a gating input for phase-two funding decisions.

Invite the MitID operator and relevant Digitaliseringsstyrelsen officials to a structured stakeholder workshop (month 5–6 of phase one) where the project presents preliminary feasibility findings and solicits formal feedback on blockers, regulatory constraints, and potential integration pathways, documenting all responses.

Establish a formal advisory board or steering committee including a representative from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, one from the MitID operator (if willing), and external security/standards experts, meeting monthly throughout the project and requiring documented positions on platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements.
levers.review: Formal engagement creates accountability and visibility, but the tension is that incumbent institutions may engage performatively—agreeing in principle to study the issue while taking no operational steps to integrate platform-neutrality into actual procurement. The options miss the structural asymmetry: the project team has incentives to claim success when any engagement occurs, while Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID have strong incentives to delay, compartmentalize, or defer the issue to future review cycles that never arrive.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Neutralization and Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Explicitly positioning platform-neutral fallback access as a continuity and resilience layer—not a replacement for MitID—allows the project to avoid direct confrontation with the incumbent operator while still advancing the core requirement. By framing fallback authentication as insurance against platform policy changes or geopolitical disruption, the project can argue that both MitID and a fallback path serve complementary public-interest functions. However, this narrative requires careful credibility work; if the fallback path is perceived as a Trojan horse for MitID displacement, the incumbent operator will mobilize institutional relationships to block it.
options: Develop a formal continuity and resilience framework document that positions fallback authentication explicitly as a certified backup access path for critical public services during platform disruptions, creating a shared interest narrative where the MitID operator benefits from reduced single-point-of-failure risk.

Engage the MitID operator as a technical stakeholder (not a decision-maker) in demonstrator design and feasibility discussions, seeking their input on how a fallback path could interoperate with MitID infrastructure without threatening its primary role, positioning the project as enhancing rather than replacing the existing ecosystem.

Propose that AltID procurement requirements include a formal continuity-of-service and platform-resilience clause that mandates testing and validation of fallback authentication paths, framing this as a standard public-sector operational requirement rather than a critique of MitID.
levers.review: Framing fallback access as continuity rather than replacement reduces incumbent resistance, but the tension is that the MitID operator will still view any fallback path as a competitive threat, since it implicit acknowledges that MitID is not sufficient for long-term resilience. The options miss the likelihood that the incumbent will cooperate tactically on technical details while simultaneously lobbying procurement bodies to exclude fallback-access requirements from formal AltID specifications, leaving the fallback path as optional rather than mandatory.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Trust Anchor Specification
levers.consequences: Defining explicit technical criteria for fallback authentication—such as FIDO2/WebAuthn over HTTP, PKCS#11 hardware tokens, or browser-based OpenID Connect—creates a measurable standard against which future procurement can be evaluated and allows the project to position demonstrators as proof of concept rather than novelty. However, specifying a trust anchor that is genuinely decoupled from app-store distribution or platform attestation requires regulatory clarity on what "platform-neutral" means under eIDAS2 Substantial level, and that clarity does not yet exist in Danish or EU implementation guidance.
options: Define a formal technical specification for fallback authentication that lists acceptable cryptographic bindings, device-binding alternatives to platform attestation, and approved authentication flows, then submit it as a standards proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the European Digital Identity Wallet working group.

Reverse-engineer the security assumptions in MitID's current platform-integrity model and translate them into vendor-agnostic requirements (device tamper-resistance, user-presence binding, revocation timeliness) that any platform or token provider could satisfy, then publish the mapping as a design principle document for future AltID procurement.

Commission an external security firm to produce a "Fallback Authentication Threat Model" that explicitly names the risks MitID's current platform dependency creates and specifies the minimum trust properties a fallback path must maintain, then use that report to justify platform-neutral language in procurement.
levers.review: The tension here is between technical rigor and regulatory clarity: a well-specified trust anchor is only useful if procurement bodies and eIDAS2 authorities actually recognize it as equivalent to or better than platform-attestation-based models. All three options risk producing a specification that is technically sound but remains sidelined as a novelty or research artifact if institutional buyers continue to defer to MitID's existing certification pathway and incumbent operator's resistance to competitive alternatives.

levers.name: Demonstrator Credibility and Scope Boundary
levers.consequences: Credible demonstrators prove that FIDO2 and OpenID Connect workflows can authenticate citizens against a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider without app-store mediation, but only if the scope is explicitly bounded—no feature parity with MitID, no attempt at production integration, and clear labeling of what is simulated vs. real. Scope creep (adding real MitID integration, citizen-facing UX polish, or feature richness) exhausts the budget, delays publication, and invites dismissal as incomplete or unsafe, whereas excessive minimalism (proof-of-concept shell scripts) risks being discounted as not credible enough to influence procurement.
options: Build two targeted demonstrators—one mobile-first (GrapheneOS with FIDO2 over HTTPS) and one browser-based (hardware token with OpenID Connect)—each authenticated against a containerized mock Danish public-sector IdP, with published security assumptions, threat model, and explicit scope boundaries, then make the demonstrators and design docs available under open-source licensing.

Develop a single, highly polished demonstrator that handles a realistic citizen authentication flow (login, consent, attribute release) end-to-end, targeting a minimal but recognizable subset of Danish public services, and undergo independent security review to establish credibility with procurement stakeholders.

Partner with a privacy-focused mobile OS project (GrapheneOS, CalyxOS) to integrate the fallback authentication path into their standard distribution, turning the demonstrator into a persistent, community-maintained reference implementation rather than a time-bound research artifact.
levers.review: The core tension is between demonstrator credibility (which requires polish and independent review) and project scope (which is already consuming 4.0 million DKK in phase two). None of the options directly address the risk that MitID operators and incumbent system integrators will dismiss the demonstrators as "not production-ready" regardless of quality—that dismissal is a political choice, not a technical one. Demonstrator credibility alone does not guarantee procurement traction without simultaneous political and regulatory pressure.

levers.name: Regulatory Alignment Sequencing
levers.consequences: Securing formal written feedback from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operator on platform-neutral requirements early (month 6–8) creates a decision record that can later be cited to justify procurement language; waiting until month 18 (phase three) means the AltID architecture may already be locked in or advancing under assumptions inherited from MitID. Early engagement signals institutional seriousness but risks premature rejection if the regulatory climate is not yet receptive, whereas late engagement bypasses early rejection but surrenders influence over architecture.
options: Initiate formal feedback-request meetings with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 3, presenting the platform-dependency risk as a continuity-of-service and supplier-concentration issue tied to the 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty strategy, and explicitly ask for written guidance on whether platform-neutral requirements are in scope for AltID.

Embed a policy advisor in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operating company during phase one to conduct informal stakeholder mapping and identify informal champions for resilience and fallback-path design, then use those relationships to frame formal requests strategically in month 7–8.

Coordinate with sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital-policy committee to lodge written inquiries to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen on platform dependency and continuity, framing the issue as a parliamentary oversight question, then use parliamentary momentum to anchor formal project-led feedback requests in month 5–6.
levers.review: The timing and framing of regulatory engagement shapes institutional receptiveness, but all three options assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is a willing interlocutor rather than a defender of incumbent MitID operator interests. If Digitaliseringsstyrelsen views the project as a threat to MitID certification or operator commercial interests, early engagement may trigger active resistance or stonewalling, whereas parliamentary inquiry may create political cover for informal dialogue but risks branding the project as adversarial rather than collaborative.

levers.name: EU Standards Leverage and Cross-Border Traction
levers.consequences: Aligning the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-path requirements with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS2 implementation guidance creates pressure that applies to Denmark through EU-level harmonization rather than domestic politics alone, and demonstrates that the issue is not parochial but reflects broader European resilience concerns. However, EU working groups move slowly, their output is often non-binding, and they are equally subject to incumbent operator lobbying; timing Danish intervention (phase three, month 18+) may be too late to influence EU wallet architecture decisions already underway.
options: Engage with the European Digital Identity Wallet working group and relevant ETSI technical committees starting in phase two, presenting platform-neutral authentication as a portability and resilience requirement under the Digital Services Act and the European Cyber Diplomacy Strategy, with backing from the project's demonstrators and feasibility report.

Commission a parallel feasibility study on platform-neutral identity in another EU member state (Estonia, Germany, or Netherlands) that is also concerned about digital sovereignty, then jointly propose a transatlantic or intra-EU working group on sovereign digital identity infrastructure that frames platform-neutrality as a shared European capacity rather than a Denmark-specific concern.

Position the project's fallback-authentication model as a solution to the EU's Digital Services Act compliance challenge: platforms must provide users with alternatives to proprietary recommendation algorithms and distribution controls, so digital identity wallets should include platform-neutral fallback paths to satisfy DSA portability requirements.
levers.review: EU-level influence is most credible when grounded in multiple member states' interests and EU legislative priorities (DSA, digital sovereignty, resilience), but the project team's bandwidth and expertise may not extend to sustained EU stakeholder management, especially if phase-two funding constraints limit travel and external coordination. Relying on EU pressure to move Danish procurement is also a slow play; if AltID procurement decisions or architectural design gates close before EU guidance materializes, the project's EU engagement becomes a post-hoc standards contribution rather than a procurement intervention.

levers.name: Civil-Society and Academic Legitimacy Chain
levers.consequences: Assembling a visible coalition of privacy advocates, cybersecurity researchers, contingency-planning professionals, and civil-society organisations creates third-party credibility that independent experts (not the project itself) view platform-neutral fallback paths as a legitimate public-sector need; this shifts the burden of proof onto incumbent operators to justify why fallback paths are unnecessary. However, coalition visibility can also trigger organized incumbent operator response, and if coalition members disagree on technical details or policy framing, the coalition becomes a liability rather than an asset.
options: Publish position statements from leading Danish and European digital-rights organizations (Internet Foundation Denmark, European Digital Rights, Access Now) explicitly calling for platform-neutral Danish digital identity, then coordinate media engagement and parliamentary testimony around the published positions rather than around the project team directly.

Organize a series of structured roundtable discussions (months 3, 7, 12) with researchers, operators, civil-society stakeholders, and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen representatives, documented and published as meeting summaries that show areas of agreement (e.g., resilience and continuity are legitimate concerns) and technical disagreement (platform-neutrality is feasible vs. impractical), then use the consensus areas to anchor policy proposals.

Establish a formal academic consortium (with Aarhus University, Copenhagen Business School, Technical University of Denmark) that commits to publishing at least three peer-reviewed papers on Danish digital identity resilience, platform dependency, and fallback authentication in months 8–24, with commitments to engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and relevant authorities as co-authors or stakeholders.
levers.review: Coalition legitimacy is real, but it is also volatile: if coalition members are drawn into direct public disputes with MitID operators or if they prioritize other policy goals over digital-identity resilience, the coalition fractures. Additionally, civil-society visibility can prompt incumbent operators to preempt criticism by proposing their own (limited) platform-diversity measures, appearing to address the concern while defending the status quo.

levers.name: Budget and Phase-Gate Contingency Structuring
levers.consequences: Explicitly structuring phase gates and contingency budgets (rather than front-loading all resources) allows the project to pivot or de-scope if institutional engagement is weak or procurement influence proves infeasible, and it enforces disciplined decision-making at months 8 and 16. However, contingency and pivot planning requires real-time assessment of progress against soft metrics (institutional receptiveness, coalition depth, procurement momentum) that are inherently ambiguous, and phased funding creates the risk that institutional funders will withdraw support in phase two or three if early momentum is unclear.
options: Structure phase-gate decisions around explicit written feedback from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator on platform-neutral requirements (yes/no to formal consideration) as a go/no-go criteria for phase two, and if feedback is negative, de-scope phase two to demonstrator publication and EU engagement only, with explicit pivot to standards and research outputs rather than procurement influence.
