Reserve 0.8 million DKK (8% of total budget) as an explicit "Institutional Engagement Contingency" pool, released only if phase one demonstrates weak formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or procurement bodies; if contingency is deployed, it funds additional policy support, legal review, or parliamentary coordination to accelerate institutional traction in phase two.

Establish a project advisory board including members from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operating company (if willing), civil-society organizations, and Folketinget, meeting quarterly to assess progress against procurement and standards outcomes; if the board identifies zero traction by month 12, the project pivots to pure research and EU standards work, with remaining budget reallocated to publication and coalition-building rather than demonstrator refinement.
levers.review: Phase gates and contingency structuring impose discipline, but they also risk creating a permission structure for de-escalation if incumbent operators or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen actively discourage platform-neutral requirements. An advisory board that includes MitID operators may provide early warning of institutional sentiment, but it can also be used to legitimize a early decision to abandon procurement influence in favor of safer, lower-stakes research output. The contingency itself is only useful if the project team has agreed in advance on what "weak traction" means and is willing to act on it.

levers.name: Fallback-Path Use-Case Framing and Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Reframing fallback authentication not as an ideological alternative to MitID but as a mandatory continuity requirement (what happens if Apple or Google restricts distribution, blocks integrity APIs, or imposes geopolitical sanctions?) converts platform-neutral requirements from a nice-to-have resilience feature into a fiduciary duty for public-sector digital planners. This narrative resonates with contingency officers, insurance and risk-management professionals, and regulators concerned with critical infrastructure. However, over-emphasizing catastrophic scenarios (geopolitical breakdown, platform withdrawal) risks being dismissed as alarmist, and incumbent operators will respond by arguing that platform duopoly is itself a resilience feature (no single point of failure).
options: Develop a scenario-based continuity-planning document ("Essential Services Under Platform Constraint") modeling what happens to Danish banking, tax, healthcare, and welfare access if Apple or Google restricts app distribution or disables integrity APIs due to regulatory action (as with TikTok or Huawei), and present it to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the Ministry of Interior as a risk-management brief rather than a project proposal.

Engage with Danish contingency-planning bodies (Defense, emergency-response agencies, critical-infrastructure regulators) to establish digital identity as a critical-infrastructure continuity requirement, then propose platform-neutral fallback paths as a standard continuity control in national digital-infrastructure risk registers and supply-chain resilience planning.

Frame platform-neutral fallback paths as a defense against platform-provider accountability gaps: if MitID integrity failures occur (API downtime, attestation spoofing, operator negligence), citizens need an alternative path to critical services; position the fallback as a quality-of-service and citizen-rights protection measure rather than as a replacement to MitID.
levers.review: The continuity narrative is powerful because it appeals to institutional risk-aversion and fiduciary duty, but it succeeds only if the framed scenarios are plausible enough to worry regulators without being so extreme that they seem speculative. If the project emphasizes geopolitical scenarios without grounding them in measurable supply-chain or platform-policy precedents, the narrative is vulnerable to dismissal as fear-mongering. Additionally, incumbent operators will argue (not without reason) that a fallback path that is only activated in catastrophic scenarios may itself become brittle or underutilized, creating false confidence or stranded investment.

levers.name: Operator-Capture Risk Narrative and Continuity Framing
levers.consequences: Reframing the issue from 'privacy vs. Apple/Google' to 'what happens if platform policy changes, distribution access is revoked, or push-notification infrastructure becomes unavailable for critical services' shifts the debate from ideology to concrete operational risk. This framing appeals to continuity-of-service stakeholders, procurement officials, and institutional risk managers who are already anxious about supplier concentration and regulatory volatility.
options: Develop and publish a formal continuity-of-service risk assessment documenting what MitID and dependent services lose if iOS or Android app distribution, integrity APIs, or push infrastructure become inaccessible or hostile for 72 hours or more, with case studies from real platform-policy disruptions elsewhere in Europe.

Commission a third-party audit of MitID's architectural dependencies on Apple and Google platform signals, producing a detailed technical report that makes the lock-in explicit and quantifies the number of critical public-sector services affected if either platform withdraws cooperation.

Engage Finanstilsynet and Sikkerhedsstyrelsen to jointly issue a formal letter to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen flagging platform-vendor concentration as a regulatory continuity risk that must be addressed in the next identity-stack evolution, framing the issue as a prudential supervision and civil-continuity concern rather than a policy preference.
levers.review: The core tension is that Danish decision-makers acknowledge supplier concentration abstractly, but see no immediate cost to inaction because services work today. This lever assumes that continuity risk language will motivate procurement change, yet it misses the institutional reality that risk assessments are often filed and shelved. The options do not address what governance mechanism or decision-gate will force procurement bodies to act on a risk report, or how to make platform-neutrality a mandatory procurement criterion rather than an advisory note.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Specification Insertion Point
levers.consequences: The eIDAS2 wallet (AltID) is currently in design and early procurement phases; inserting platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into its technical specification and procurement documents now will shape supplier bids and architectural choices before design hardens. Missing this window means the same Apple/Google dependencies replicate into the next generation of identity infrastructure.
options: Prepare a detailed technical amendment to the AltID procurement brief specifying that at least one certified authentication path must not depend on iOS or Android app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push notifications, with explicit reference to FIDO2/WebAuthn and hardware-token fallback options, and submit it formally to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by month 12.

Work with Danish procurement law specialists to draft model language for AltID and related identity contracts requiring bidders to demonstrate platform-neutral and fallback-capable architectures, then place this language in a publicly available toolkit that procurement bodies and ministries can adopt without re-negotiation.

Embed platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into the AltID conformity-assessment criteria and eIDAS2 certification checklist used by Danish and EU validators, ensuring that certified wallets must prove their independence from exclusive platform reliance or face decertification risk.
levers.review: The lever correctly identifies AltID as a critical decision window, but assumes that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will actively solicit or be receptive to external procurement input. In practice, procurement documents are often locked before public consultation, and inserting new requirements mid-cycle creates cost and schedule friction that procurement bodies resist. The options do not account for how to make platform-neutrality a contractual obligation rather than an optional nice-to-have, or how to prevent suppliers from creating a token fallback that is technically compliant but operationally orphaned and unsupported.

levers.name: Hardware-Token and Browser-Based Escape Hatch
levers.consequences: Developing a working non-production demonstrator that authenticates Danish citizens using a hardware token (e.g., YubiKey) or browser-based WebAuthn flow decouples authentication from mobile OS platforms entirely and provides proof that critical public-sector access does not require Apple or Google mediation. This shifts the conversation from 'is it possible?' to 'we have code and users have tried it.'
options: Build and test a browser-based FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication demonstrator integrated with a simulated Danish identity provider, allowing users on any device (desktop, older phone, tablet) with a web browser to authenticate without relying on platform-specific app stores or device-integrity APIs, and publish performance and usability data.

Develop a USB hardware-token authentication path (using FIDO2-compatible tokens such as YubiKey or Titan) that works with a simplified Nordic Public Sector digital identity mock-up, demonstrating that citizens without modern smartphones or those who distrust app platforms have a certified fallback.

Create a privacy-respecting Android authenticator application running on GrapheneOS or /e/OS that demonstrates FIDO2 and OpenID Connect authentication against a mock Danish identity provider without depending on Google Play Services, Play Integrity API, or proprietary push notifications, then publish architectural documentation on how this design avoids platform lock-in.
levers.review: This lever assumes that a working demonstrator will move procurement debates from theoretical objections to practical feasibility. However, a non-production prototype does not address the integration burden on banks, healthcare portals, and tax authorities, nor does it solve the cultural preference for mobile-app convenience. The options produce evidence but do not include a path to pilot adoption in real services, so the demonstrators risk remaining demonstrations rather than becoming operational alternatives that citizens and operators actually trust and use.

levers.name: Academic and Civil-Society Third-Party Legitimacy
levers.consequences: Publishing technical findings and policy proposals through academic and civil-society partners (universities, digital-rights organisations, international security bodies) rather than solely through project channels creates peer-reviewed credibility and political distance from vendor-hostile framing. This makes platform-neutrality appear as a rational institutional position, not a niche complaint.
options: Publish the feasibility study and demonstrator technical findings in peer-reviewed security and digital-governance journals, and present at international conferences (e.g., EIC, IEEE S&P, NDSS) to establish that platform-neutral Danish digital identity is a recognized research problem with credible solutions, not a fringe idea.

Partner with established civil-society organisations such as Internetfonden, Privacy International, and Access Now to co-author policy briefs and co-host public workshops in Copenhagen on digital sovereignty and platform resilience, ensuring that the message appears to come from trusted independent voices rather than from a single project.

Engage academic digital-identity research networks in Denmark, Germany, and Sweden to form a working group that collectively documents platform-dependency risks and fallback-access architectures across Northern Europe, making this a multi-country institutional issue rather than a Denmark-only agenda.
levers.review: The lever relies on the assumption that academic and civil-society credibility will influence procurement and standards decisions, yet policy makers often dismiss academic research as out of touch with operational constraints. The options do not specify how to connect research publication to actual procurement bodies or standards committees, nor do they address the time lag between publication and institutional adoption, which may exceed the 24-month project horizon.

levers.name: EU Wallet Harmonization and Standards Lock-In
levers.consequences: The European Digital Identity Wallet (EUDIW) framework is under active development and will set the template for all member-state identity implementations, including Denmark's AltID. Embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into EU wallet standards now will create a binding constraint that Danish procurement bodies and operators must respect, turning supplier dependence into a technical and legal non-compliance issue.
options: Engage directly with the EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and eIDAS2 standards bodies to propose specific technical requirements for platform-neutral and fallback-capable authentication paths, backed by the project's feasibility report and demonstrators, and secure formal inclusion in the EUDIW implementation guidelines.

Coordinate with other European digital-sovereignty initiatives (France's MoneID, Germany's Gaiax, Sweden's e-ID coalition) to form a multi-country advocacy coalition that demands EU-level platform-neutrality and resilience requirements in the EUDIW standard, making platform lock-in a supra-national compliance issue rather than a national procurement preference.

Participate in and shape the EU's Digital Europe programme and Horizon Europe funding calls to ensure that future wallet and identity-infrastructure grants explicitly require platform-neutral and fallback-capable architectures, creating financial incentives for supplier-independent design rather than Apple/Google mediation.
levers.review: This lever correctly identifies the EU wallet framework as a critical leverage point, but assumes that EU standards-setting bodies will adopt platform-neutrality language when they currently do not prioritize it. The options do not account for the fact that EU standards processes are slow (12–24 months), that many member states and large vendors have entrenched interests in app-based identity, and that 'platform-neutral' may be interpreted narrowly as 'works on both iOS and Android' rather than 'avoids platform dependence altogether.' The options also do not specify how to ensure that platform-neutrality requirements in EU standards are enforced during member-state implementation, rather than treated as optional guidance.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Co-Option
levers.consequences: MitID operator (NemLog-in) and major identity-dependent services (banks, healthcare, tax) may resist fallback-access requirements if they perceive them as operational burden or cost. Engaging them early as co-designers of fallback architectures (rather than as obstacles) transforms them from blockers into implementers and creates buy-in for continuity planning that benefits them as well.
options: Establish a formal Working Group on Identity Resilience and Continuity with NemLog-in, major Danish banks, and healthcare authorities, wherein the project presents the fallback-authentication demonstrators and invites participants to identify operational and integration pathways that do not require wholesale replacement of MitID, but rather add a certified alternative access path for scenarios where platform availability is compromised.

Commission NemLog-in to conduct an internal feasibility assessment of integrating FIDO2/WebAuthn and hardware-token fallback paths into MitID's certification and operation models, positioning the project's demonstrators as a proof-of-concept that informs NemLog-in's own roadmap rather than as external critique.

Offer Danish banks and healthcare providers a pilot programme in which they integrate the project's hardware-token and browser-based fallback authentication paths into their identity-verification workflows for non-critical or supplementary services, generating operational data and user feedback that mitigates institutional reluctance to adopt fallback paths in production.
levers.review: This lever assumes that incumbent operators will engage constructively if positioned as partners rather than as targets of pressure. However, MitID is a profitable, conservative system, and NemLog-in has institutional and commercial incentives to maintain Apple/Google dependence if it reduces their liability and operational burden. The options do not address how to handle the likely scenario in which NemLog-in agrees to engage but uses the process to delay or dilute platform-neutrality requirements, or how to escalate if engagement stalls.

levers.name: Regulatory Mandate Trigger and Compliance Ratchet
levers.consequences: If platform-neutral and fallback-access requirements are embedded in Danish financial regulations (via Finanstilsynet), healthcare system standards (via Sundhedsstyrelsen), or formal government digital-governance mandates, they become mandatory compliance items rather than optional policy suggestions. This creates a bottom-up institutional pressure that procurement bodies cannot ignore without explaining regulatory non-compliance.
options: Work with Finanstilsynet to embed identity-system resilience and platform-neutrality requirements into the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) implementation guidance and bank-specific security regulations, making fallback-access capability a formal supervisory expectation for all banks using digital identity for customer authentication.

Secure a formal government directive or digital-strategy update (in line with the 2026–2029 Digital Welfare Strategy) that mandates all public-sector digital-identity systems procured or certified after 2027 must include platform-neutral and certified fallback-access paths, enforceable via audit and decertification.

Engage Sundhedsstyrelsen and regional health authorities to adopt platform-neutral and fallback-access requirements as a binding criterion in all future healthcare identity and authorization system procurements, positioning digital sovereignty and continuity of care as health-sector imperatives rather than IT preferences.
levers.review: This lever attempts to use regulatory and supervisory authority to override procurement inertia, yet it assumes that regulators will prioritize platform-neutrality when they currently do not and may face political or commercial pressure to avoid imposing costly requirements on operators. The options do not account for the lengthy timelines of regulatory change (12–36 months) or the possibility that incumbent operators and large platforms will lobby against compliance requirements. The options also do not specify what enforcement mechanism or penalty structure will ensure that compliance mandates are actual rather than aspirational.

Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead of 8–14 weeks per site that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Migrating the rendering pipeline to GPU compute cuts frame time from 12 ms to 4 ms in benchmarks, yet every asset authored for the CPU path must be re-profiled, and the team has no GPU debugging toolchain — the migration cost is hidden in artist-hours, not engineering-hours."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")
   - NO starting review_lever with "The options", "These options", "The lever", or "These levers" as the grammatical subject — name a specific mechanism, constraint, or risk instead

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
